executive_summary: High remittance fees for migrant workers sending money from the US to Central America represent a well-documented, painful problem with extensive complaints, multiple workarounds, and a saturated market of paid alternatives.
score_reasoning: Score 78/100 — Complaint Volume: 13/20 (35+ unique complaint threads across Trustpilot, ConsumerAffairs, Quora, news articles, and institutional reports). Complaint Recency: 17/20 (85%+ from last 24 months including World Bank Q1 2025 data, 2024 Inter-American Dialogue report, ongoing Trustpilot reviews). Pain Intensity Signals: 14/20 (17+ pain markers including 5+ quantified costs such as 19.7% commission on $600 transfer, $45 bank wire fees, 6.49% global average, 14.55% bank average; no explicit WTP for crypto alternative specifically). Workaround Evidence: 16/20 (7 distinct workarounds including digital-only MTOs, crypto wallets with stablecoins, comparison shopping across providers, using bank account instead of credit card). Paid Alternatives: 18/20 (16+ paid alternatives with reviews including Western Union 107K+ reviews, Remitly 90K+ reviews, Xoom 23K+ reviews, plus Wise, MoneyGram, Ria, Pangea, Intermex, BOSS Revolution, Sharemoney, Paysend, Común, MAJORITY, Wells Fargo, Taptap Send, Sendwave). Total: 13 + 17 + 14 + 16 + 18 = 78.

data.problem_statement: Migrant workers in the US sending remittances to Central America lose significant portions of their transfers (averaging 6.49% globally, up to 14.55% via banks) to fees and hidden exchange rate markups from traditional services like Western Union and MoneyGram, reducing funds that represent up to 60% of recipient household income.
data.target_user: Migrant workers in the US (primarily from Guatemala, Honduras, El Salvador, Nicaragua) sending regular remittances to family members in Central America.

current_solutions.solution: Paying via bank account instead of card to reduce fees

data.evidence_summary: 35+ unique complaint threads found across Trustpilot (Western Union 107K+ reviews, Remitly 90K+, Xoom 23K+), ConsumerAffairs, Quora, institutional reports (World Bank, UN, IMF, Inter-American Dialogue), and news outlets (Fortune, Economist, NerdWallet). Pattern: widespread frustration with fee opacity, hidden exchange rate markups, and high total cost of traditional remittance services. The problem is institutionally recognized — the UN SDG target is to reduce costs below 3%, yet global average remains 6.49%. Central America corridors are among the highest-volume from the US, with remittances representing up to 26% of GDP for recipient countries.
